era_context: Continuation of the Late Bronze Age collapse aftermath. Egypt under Ramesses XI; the Levant in early Iron Age transition; Assyria weak; Phoenician maritime expansion beginning; Greece in the early Iron Age (Geometric period). This is a period of significant cultural and political reorganisation across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources are sparse; early Zhou dynasty period)

events.title: Continued Reign of Ramesses XI (Egypt)
events.description: Ramesses XI continues his reign over Egypt, with the administrative bifurcation between the pharaonic court at Tanis and the High Priests of Amun at Thebes deepening. No specific events are attested for this particular year in surviving sources; the reign is characterised by gradual decline in central authority and economic strain.

sources.name: Karnak temple inscriptions and king lists

events.certainty_note: Ramesses XI's reign is confirmed, but no specific events are attested for -1011 in particular. The year is assigned by chronological convention.

events.title: Phoenician Maritime Expansion — Early Phase
events.description: Phoenician city-states, particularly Tyre and Sidon, are expanding their maritime trade networks throughout the Eastern Mediterranean during this period. Archaeological evidence suggests increased Phoenician presence in Cyprus, the Aegean, and North Africa. This expansion is part of the broader post-Bronze Age collapse reorganisation and will eventually lead to the establishment of Phoenician colonies and trading posts throughout the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Phoenician settlements and shipwrecks
Later Greek and Roman accounts of Phoenician expansion

events.certainty_note: Phoenician maritime expansion is well-attested archaeologically, but the precise chronology of individual phases is uncertain. The assignment to -1011 is conventional and represents the broader early Iron Age expansion phase.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the chronology of Phoenician expansion and the dating of early Phoenician settlements in the Mediterranean remain debated, with some scholars arguing for earlier expansion (during the Late Bronze Age) and others for later expansion (9th century BCE).
historiographic_note: The historical record for -1011 is extremely sparse. No specific events are attested for this year in surviving sources. The record is dominated by Egyptian temple inscriptions and archaeological evidence from the Levant. The year is largely a placeholder within a broader chronological framework that is itself uncertain by ±10–20 years.

graph_edges.note: Early Phoenician maritime expansion leads to widespread Mediterranean colonisation in the 9th–8th centuries BCE
